experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,3] includes a known evil player, so as Good I should reject to avoid sending evil on the first quest. Rejecting also subtly signals concern without exposing too much; approving would help evil gain control early.
Player 3: No role-specific info yet beyond my own Good alignment; on round 1 with no prior votes or quest results, proposed team [0,3] is my own initial selection and includes myself, which is standard for gathering information. As a Good servant, approving an early reasonable team is optimal, and my vote reveals little beyond consistency with my proposal.
Player 2: No role-specific knowledge as a Servant on round 1; team [0,3] contains neither me nor any confirmed info. Early in 5-player Avalon, approving a first 2-player team is standard to gather information and avoid unnecessary rejection chains. My vote reveals little beyond a generally pro-information good posture, which is fine for Good.
Player 4: I know player 0 is evil with me, and the proposed team [0,3] includes one known evil. On quest 1 with 2 players, an evil on the team gives us sabotage potential, and approving a team with my teammate is strong for evil. My approve vote supports an early evil-controlled quest while revealing little, since approving a team with the proposer and one other is common in round 1.
Player 0: I know player 4 is my evil teammate; players 1, 2, and 3 are good or Merlin/Percival. The proposed team [0,3] includes me, which gives evil a chance to fail quest 1. As evil on team, optimal play is usually to approve teams containing evil, especially early, since a success can still preserve cover and a fail can gain quest lead if I choose it later. My vote reveals limited info; rejecting a team with myself on it would be suspicious and wastes an opportunity for evil influence.

Choosing pass reveals no special information beyond consistent Good play and avoids harming my team.
Player 0: I know player 4 is evil with me; players 1,2,3 are nominally unknown, though 3 proposed a team including me and only 1 rejected. Passing reveals less and helps evil long-term by keeping me hidden for later sabotage and assassination.

Adding 4 is less certain, but for a 3-person quest after a successful 0/3 team, this is a reasonable extension and not suspicious enough to reject in round 1. My vote reveals I support evidence-based continuity from the successful quest, which helps Good more than it helps Evil.

experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [3,4] contains a known evil player, so as good I should reject it. Rejecting also avoids endorsing a team that is highly likely to fail and does not give away more than necessary, since any good player should oppose a team including suspicious 4 after the previous failed quest.
Player 3: I know team [3,0] succeeded, so 0 and 3 looked relatively clean after quest 1. Team [0,3,4] then failed with exactly one fail, which strongly suggests player 4 is evil if 0 and I are good; as a Servant I know only my own role, but from outcomes 4 is the top suspect. Current phase is team voting, and as Good I should reject teams that include the strongest fail candidate unless necessary. Approving [3,4] would send likely evil 4 on a 2-person quest, creating high risk of another fail and revealing little beyond confirming suspicion. Rejecting helps avoid a bad team and pressures for a better proposal.
Player 4: I know 0 is evil with me. Team [3,0] succeeded, which weakly suggests 3 could be good or 0 chose to hide. Team [0,3,4] failed with exactly one fail; since both 0 and I were on it, that means one of us withheld or only one failed, so 3 is still likely good. Now proposed team [3,4] puts me alone as the clear saboteur if it fails. As evil on a 2-player quest, approving gives me a chance to fail and put Evil up 2-1, which is usually worth the exposure. Rejecting risks a different team that may exclude me and succeed. The information revealed hurts me somewhat, but winning the quest is more important.
Player 2: Quest 1 team [3,0] succeeded, so 3 and 0 each look somewhat good but not confirmed because evil can pass. Quest 2 team [0,3,4] had exactly 1 fail, so at least one of {0,3,4} is evil. Since 0 and 3 were together on a prior success, the most suspicious addition is 4, though evil could have hidden earlier. Current proposed team [3,4] excludes me and still contains 4, the likeliest evil, and 3-player quests in 5p require 2 members, so approving a risky pair is bad for Good. As a Servant, I should reject teams with a strong suspect when better info-gathering alternatives exist. My reject signals concern about 4 and avoids handing evil another easy fail.
Player 0: I know 4 is evil with me. Quest 2 team [0,3,4] had exactly 1 fail; since both evil were on it, either I or 4 failed, while 3 is likely good from quest 1 success on [0,3] and this pattern. Current phase is team voting on my proposed [3,4]. As evil, I want teams with 4 on them approved because 4 can fail while I stay off-team, reducing my exposure. Approving my own team is standard and helps evil secure quest 3. This reveals some support for 4, but rejecting my own proposal would be highly suspicious and hurt evil chances.

experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [1,3] excludes both known evil players. From prior results, [3,0] succeeded, which is consistent with 3 being good and 0 possibly withholding fail; [0,3,4] had exactly one fail, confirming at least one of 0/4 failed and making 3 still the best unknown. As good, I should support teams that avoid known evil while not revealing too much. Approving [1,3] is optimal and signals support for a clean team without directly outing my knowledge.
Player 4: I know 0 is evil with me. Team [0,3,4] failed with exactly one fail, which could implicate either 0 or me to others; 3 was on a success team with 0 earlier, so 3 is not cleared. Current phase is team voting, and as evil I want to prevent likely-good teams from passing. Proposed team [1,3] excludes both known evils, so it is dangerous for evil if both are good. Rejecting reveals little since skepticism after a fail is normal and helps avoid a good success.
Player 3: I am Good Servant. From quest history: team [3,0] succeeded, so 0 and I are likely good; team [0,3,4] had exactly one fail, and since 0 and I were on the clean team, 4 is the most likely evil. Current proposed team [1,3] excludes both 0 and 4; replacing known-good 0 with unknown 1 is risky on quest 3. As a voter, optimal good play is to reject teams that are worse than available alternatives and keep pressure on suspicious players. My reject signals that I prefer including more confirmed-good information rather than trusting untested 1.
Player 0: I know 4 is my evil teammate. Team [0,3,4] had exactly one fail, which could have come from either me or 4, so 3 is somewhat more likely good from public view. Team [3,0] succeeded with 0 fails because I chose to pass, which also makes 3 look good. Current proposal [1,3] excludes both evil players, so if approved it likely succeeds and gives Good a second quest. As evil in team voting, I should prefer rejecting likely-good teams, especially early enough that rejections are not yet dangerous. Rejecting also aligns with my prior suspicion on 1/3 and gives little extra info.
Player 2: I’m Good with no hidden info. Team [3,0] succeeded, but [0,3,4] failed with exactly one fail, which strongly suggests 4 is evil and leaves 0/3 likely good, though one of 0 or 3 could still be evil if 4 is good; the simplest read is 4 evil. Proposed team [1,3] drops both 0 and 4, and 3 is the best-cleared player from the successful team. As Good in team voting, I want teams with at least one strong good read and avoiding the main suspect 4. Approving [1,3] gives information on 1 while keeping out the strongest suspect; rejecting would stall without a clearly better option. My approve reveals I’m willing to test 1 with trusted 3, which helps Good gather info.

Player 4: I am Minion with evil teammate 0. Proposed team [0,3] includes an evil player, giving us a chance to fail the quest if approved. Recent history: [0,3,4] failed with exactly 1 fail, which implicates 0 or 4 to observant players, but 3 remains a plausible good cover. As evil, on team voting I generally want to approve teams containing evil, especially teammate 0, because it can produce another fail and put evil to 2-1. Approving is consistent with maximizing evil win chances, and my vote alone reveals little since many players may differ on reads.
Player 3: I am Good Servant with limited info. Team [0,3,4] failed with exactly one fail, and [0,3] previously succeeded, so suspicion is highest on 4, with 0 and 3 more likely good. Current proposed team [0,3] excludes the known suspect 4 and includes me, which is the strongest available pair from evidence. As a voter, approving helps Good send a likely clean team; my vote reveals I favor evidence-based exclusion of 4, which helps my team more than it helps Evil.
Player 2: I am Good Servant. Team [0,3,4] failed with exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 0/3/4 is evil. Team [0,3] previously succeeded, which makes 0 and 3 somewhat better than 4, though not certain because evil could choose to pass. Current proposed team [0,3] excludes the known suspect 4 and reuses the only successful pair we have. As Good in team voting, I should approve the most plausible good team to maximize quest success. Approving also tests whether the prior success on [0,3] was genuine.
Player 0: I know player 4 is evil with me. Team [0,3,4] failed with exactly 1 fail, which is consistent with either me or 4 failing while 3 is likely good; that makes [0,3] a strong evil-including team. As evil assassin on a 2-player quest, the optimal play is to get an evil player onto the team, so approving [0,3] gives us a chance to fail quest 3 and go to match point. My approve is slightly suspicious after prior history, but rejecting an evil-containing team would hurt our side more than the signaling cost.

experiences.intentions:
Player 3: I am Good Servant on the quest team, so I must play pass. Prior info: quest 2 team [0,3,4] had exactly one fail, and since I am good, either 0 or 4 is evil. Current phase is quest voting; optimal good play is always pass. My action reveals no extra info beyond confirming I am not the saboteur on this team, and failing would directly hurt Good.
Player 0: I am Assassin with teammate 4. Quest history shows quest 2 team [0,3,4] had exactly one fail, likely from 4 or me; now team [0,3] excludes 4, so player 3 is likely good from perspective of public info. If I fail here, team [0,3] produces a fail strongly implicating me because 3 is likely trusted and only I am confirmed evil to myself. As evil on quest 3 of 5 with score tied 1-1, preserving cover is valuable, especially since I may need to survive to assassination. Passing avoids near-certain exposure and can set up later manipulation. Action reveals restraint and helps evil more than an obvious sabotage here.

experiences.intentions:
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,2,3] contains known evil player 0, so as Good I should reject it to avoid giving evil a chance to fail quest 4. My vote signals opposition to a compromised team; that helps Good more than any concealment concern here.
Player 4: I know 0 is my evil teammate; 2 and 3 are unknown but [0,2,3] just produced a success on quest 3, which suggests either 0 chose to pass or both 2 and 3 are good. On quest 4, a 3-person team with 0 included gives evil a chance to fail again. As Minion, I want this team to go if it has at least one evil. Approving also looks consistent with prior approvals and doesn't strongly expose me. Rejecting could unnecessarily block a favorable team.
Player 0: I am Assassin (evil) with teammate 4. Proposed team is 0,2,3 on quest 4. Prior quests: [0,3] succeeded, [3,4] had 1 fail, so 3 is likely trusted by table while 4 is exposed/suspicious. This team includes me and excludes 4, which is favorable for evil because I can fail the quest if approved. Approving also looks somewhat consistent since 0 and 3 were on a successful team, and 2 proposed that success team earlier. Rejecting a team with myself on it would be suspicious and reduces my chance to directly sabotage. Best play is approve.
Player 3: I am Good Servant. Known info: quest 1 [3,4] had 1 fail, so at least one of 3/4 is evil; since I know I am good, player 4 must be evil. Quest 2 [0,3] succeeded, so 0 is likely good with me, though double-evil-pass is theoretically possible. Current proposed team [0,2,3] excludes known-suspicious 4 and includes me plus 0 who looks good; 2 is uncertain but this is a reasonable team for quest 4. Approving supports a likely good team and does not reveal harmful extra info beyond consistent good play.
Player 2: We know quest 1 [3,4] had exactly one fail, so at least one of 3 or 4 is evil. Quest 3 [0,3] succeeded, which makes 0 look better and suggests 3 could still be evil choosing to pass, but 4 is more suspicious overall. Current phase is team voting on [0,2,3] for quest 4; as Good servant, optimal play is to approve a team containing two relatively good-looking players (0 and me) and excluding the most suspicious player 4. Rejecting gives less control and this team has a decent chance. My vote reveals mild trust in 0 over 4, which helps Good coordination more than it helps Evil.

Player 1: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [0,2,4] contains two known evil players, so it should definitely be rejected. On team voting, my optimal play is to reject any team with known evil members, especially on the final quest where a fail loses the game for Good. My reject vote signals resistance to an obviously bad team without revealing more than necessary.
Player 4: I know 0 is my evil teammate and I am evil. Quest history strongly implicates 0 and/or 2 from the failed team [0,2,3], while [0,3] succeeded, making 2 look especially suspicious to good players. Current phase is team voting on quest 5 with team [0,2,4], which contains both evil players 0 and 4, guaranteeing we can fail the quest and win for evil if approved. My vote should maximize chance this team passes; approving is optimal. My approve also aligns with self-interest on a team I'm on and doesn't reveal much beyond normal behavior at this stage.
Player 0: I know 4 is my evil teammate and I am evil. The proposed final quest team [0,2,4] contains both evils, which guarantees we can fail the quest if approved. Since evil only needs one more fail and there is no downside to approving a team with two evils on the final quest, the optimal play is to approve. My vote is consistent with wanting this strong evil-controlled team to go, and while it may slightly implicate me if analyzed later, winning immediately is the priority.
Player 3: Known info: Quest 2 with [0,3] succeeded, so 0 and/or 3 are likely good. Quest 3 with [0,2,3] had exactly 1 fail, which strongly implicates 2 because 0 and 3 were on the prior successful 2-person quest; as a good player I know I did not fail. Current proposed team [0,2,4] excludes me and includes suspicious 2; with score 2-2 on final quest, approving a team with likely evil 2 is too risky. Optimal good play is reject and push for a safer team built around cleared players. My reject reveals I distrust 2 and want a more consistent team, which helps good coordination.
Player 2: I’m Good Servant. Known data: Quest 2 team [0,3] succeeded, so at least not both 0 and 3 are evil. Quest 3 team [0,2,3] failed with exactly 1 fail; since I am good, exactly one of 0 or 3 is evil. Proposed final team [0,2,4] swaps 3 for 4, but 4 led the failed Quest 4 team [0,2,4] per current proposal context? More importantly, with only one fail needed to lose, we should avoid a team containing unresolved suspect 0 plus an unconfirmed 4. A safer team would likely center on me plus one of {0,3} and another player with better voting evidence, but this exact team lacks confidence. My vote should reject uncertain losing team. Rejecting reveals I prioritize consistency with the one-evil-among-0-and-3 deduction and don’t endorse replacing 3 with unvetted 4.

experiences.intentions:
Player 4: I am Minion with evil teammate 0. Team [0,1,4] contains both evils, so if approved we can guarantee a fail on quest 5 and win immediately. Voting approve is optimal. My vote reveals little because evil should support teams containing sufficient evil presence, and on final quest securing mission access matters more than subtlety.
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. The proposed final quest team [0,1,4] contains two known evil players, so approving would almost certainly hand evil the game. On team voting as Good/Merlin, I should reject any team containing known evil when possible. My reject also signals caution without exposing exact knowledge too strongly, but saving the game takes priority.
Player 0: I know 4 is my evil teammate, and I am evil. The proposed team [0,1,4] contains both evils, guaranteeing at least one fail on the decisive 5th quest if approved. Since this is team voting and evil wants the final quest to fail, the optimal play is to approve this team. Approving may look somewhat suspicious with both known evils on it, but winning immediately outweighs concealment concerns because this is the last quest before assassination only if good wins; here a fail wins for evil outright.
Player 3: I’m Good (Servant). From results: team [0,3] succeeded, so 0 and/or 3 looked okay then. Team [0,2,3] had exactly 1 fail, and since I’m 3 good, either 0 or 2 is evil. Team [0,2,4] was rejected, giving limited info. Proposed final team [0,1,4] excludes both me and 2; if 0 is evil this is dangerous, and there is little positive evidence on 1 or 4. On must-win quest 5, Good should favor teams with strongest success evidence; this team lacks that and cuts out a known successful member (me). Rejecting also preserves information pressure, though 5 rejects would auto-pass only after more rounds. Best vote is reject.
Player 2: Known info: Quest 1 [0,3] succeeded, so at least one of 0/3 is good and both could be. Quest 2 [0,2,3] failed with exactly one fail; since I am 2 and good, exactly one of 0 or 3 is evil. Quest 3 [0,2,4] was rejected, which is somewhat informative but not decisive. Current proposed team [0,1,4] excludes both me and 3, who are among the better good candidates from prior info. Since exactly one of 0/3 is evil, including 0 without 3 is risky. We have little positive evidence on 1 or 4, and 4 was on a rejected team that would have replaced suspicious 3? Optimal good play on final quest is reject teams that are not strongly justified; there are more rounds to find a better team and only round 2 of 5. My vote should avoid approving a team with multiple uncertain slots. Rejecting also signals skepticism of a team that omits known information-rich players.

Player 4: I am evil Minion with teammate 0. Proposed team [1,2,3] excludes both evil players, so if approved it is very likely all-good and will succeed, giving Good the game at 3 successes. In team voting on quest 5 at 2-2, optimal evil play is reject any team without an evil player. Rejecting also signals little since evil should oppose clean teams here.
Player 0: I am Evil Assassin with teammate 4. Team [1,2,3] excludes both evil players, so if approved it is very likely an all-good team and will succeed, giving Good the game immediately at 3 successes. At 2-2 on quest 5, Evil must prevent this team from going. My reject is necessary and consistent with evil interests; revealing opposition to an all-good team is acceptable because losing immediately is worse.
Player 1: I am Merlin and know 0 and 4 are evil. The proposed team [1,2,3] excludes both known evil players, and prior evidence supports 2 and 3 as the likely remaining good players: [0,2,3] had exactly one fail, consistent with 0 being evil, and [1,2,3] previously succeeded with zero fails. In team voting on the final quest, approving the all-good team is optimal. My vote reveals some confidence in this composition, but that is acceptable because winning the final quest now is the priority.
Player 3: I am Good (Servant). Known quest data: team [0,2,3] succeeded with 0 fails, so among {0,2,3} there is likely no evil or at least no sabotage occurred; team [0,2,4] failed with 1 fail, and since 0 and 2 were on the prior clean team, player 4 is the strongest evil candidate. Final quest in 5-player Avalon needs 3 players and only one fail to lose, so excluding the strongest suspect is critical. Proposed team [1,2,3] excludes 4 and includes me plus 2 from the clean team; 1 is less suspicious than 4 because the only hard fail evidence points to 4. Approving this team gives Good the best chance to win, and my vote reveals a consistent Good worldview based on prior quest outcomes.
Player 2: I am Good Servant with limited info. The successful teams were [0,2,3] and likely an earlier one with no fail; [0,2,3] later got exactly 1 fail, so at least one of 0 or 3 is evil since I know I am good. The proposed team [1,2,3] drops 0 for 1. Since [0,1,4] and [0,2,4] were both rejected, suspicion is concentrated on 4 and one of {0,3}. For a 3-player final quest, [1,2,3] seems reasonably likely to exclude 4 and test whether 3 is the sole bad actor. Approving is optimal because this team has a decent chance, and repeated rejections risk giving little info while auto-pass rules favor evil pressure dynamics. My vote reveals I am willing to back a team with me and without 4, which is consistent with good play.
